elements.text: I guess not.

text:
Hi all! I’ve been diving into CRDT tech lately to build local-first collaborative apps. So far I had a great experience with Yjs - but I found the API a bit cumbersome.
I’ve been working a new open source library to make it _really easy_ to work with state that’s shared across multiple users and that syncs automatically. It supports React, Vue, Svelte, or plain javascript.

By using Javascript Proxies, the API looks just like a regular app, e.g.: `store.todos.push( { completed: true, title: "write code" } );` - and the Reactive Programming model makes it effortless to listen to changes.

Anway, I just put it live on <https://syncedstore.org/> - would really welcome your feedback!

PS: it’s also on HN (see “show” page), comments / votes there are always appreciated to help a fellow FoCer ;)

text: Simple RPC-style interaction covers most use cases I can personally think of. The role of your serverless runtime would then be to make the code called via RPC debuggable. That looks useful in the abstract. Unfortunately, I can't really say what features would be required to make it useful in real life.

elements.text: Simple RPC-style interaction covers most use cases I can personally think of. The role of your serverless runtime would then be to make the code called via RPC debuggable. That looks useful in the abstract. Unfortunately, I can't really say what features would be required to make it useful in real life.

text: Another question I wonder about: how does your project differ from other interactive systems being used on the server via some remote control? Thinking of Common Lisp image managed from Emacs (SLIME) from a desktop machines, or of Smalltalk systems (e.g. via TelePharo, <https://github.com/pharo-ide/TelePharo>). Are you doing the same but in JavaScript? Or is there a more fundamental difference I am missing?

attachments.fallback: GitHub: GitHub - pharo-ide/TelePharo: Tools to manage and develop remote Pharo images

). Are you doing the same but in JavaScript? Or is there a more fundamental difference I am missing?

text:
Why webcode is different
1. a notebook is both front end and the backend and the technical documentation and the code repository and runtime. True seamless code sharing. No additional infra required. corrorally -> libraries can be packaged as frontend and backend too
2. The environment is 100% browser. No tools to install. No "pip install", no "webpack", no compiling. You can do it all from a chromebook or mobile device, for instance
3. It takes about 40ms for code changes to become visible remotely. Compared to 2 mins for lambda. The inner development loop is supercharged. You can develop live in prod. The iteration speed is incredible, partly due to instant deploys, partly due to Observable hot code reloading No setup required, its like that out of the box.
4. True production debugging using vanilla browser tools. No simulacrum of a debugger. It's just the normal debugger everyone already uses, which means other debugging technology can be layered on top without problems (e.g. time travel debugging <https://www.replay.io/>)
This is built on Observablehq, so it gets all its FoC features. Literate programming, Commenting, 1-click forking, version control, Functional Reactive Programming env, partial recompilation etc. No need for Github or git, remember, this is 100% browser based, you get all this on any device that supports a web browser. You can fix and deploy things on your mobile phone.

You can manage a whole microservice, including technical documentation and project management, in a single page of web editable code. For instance, this twitter bot is hosted on webcode <https://observablehq.com/@endpointservices/twitter-bot>. This is a whole IndieAuth oauth server. (<https://observablehq.com/@endpointservices/auth>)

root.text:
Hi, I have built a serverless runtime for <http://observablehq.com|observablehq.com> called <https://webcode.run>. This extends Observable's reactive notebooks to the backend. Its unique features are instant deploys, and it can redirect traffic to your browser, so you can do devtool or console.log debugging against production traffic. It's a unique way of sharing code across frontend and backend.

My aim is to create a serverless environment that is a pleasure to use as a developer. I am really desperate for feedback, so if you give it a go let know the positives and negatives.

text:
So if you want your own Oauth server, its  1-click fork, change the config to point to your Firebase, and its live. There is no deploy step required, a notebook becomes infra instantly.

I guess the overall draw is it's a single tool to do everything (<http://Observablehq.com|Observablehq.com> being the single tool, webcode is just filling a missing gap)

text: If we compare computing systems to the actual concepts of language (natural) and writing, we can immediately see the shortcoming of talking solely about "programming languages"

elements.text: If we compare computing systems to the actual concepts of language (natural) and writing, we can immediately see the shortcoming of talking solely about "programming languages"

text: Programming "languages" are not very good on their own and lack the most important feature of true natural language, which is that you get it for free no matter what

elements.text: Programming "languages" are not very good on their own and lack the most important feature of true natural language, which is that you get it for free no matter what

text: In this sense, programming languages are more like writing, which has to be learned over a sustained period of time, and which is the symbolic representation of language and linguistic thought.

elements.text: In this sense, programming languages are more like writing, which has to be learned over a sustained period of time, and which is the symbolic representation of language and linguistic thought.

text: All of this exists within an environment that gives it context and which enables its use, in standard or novel ways

elements.text: All of this exists within an environment that gives it context and which enables its use, in standard or novel ways

text: What I'm saying is that it's not enough to talk of programming languages on their own. It will get you nowhere. You have to talk about computing environments

elements.text: What I'm saying is that it's not enough to talk of programming languages on their own. It will get you nowhere. You have to talk about computing environments

text: Doing most of your computing in a system that still uses a teletype metaphor at its core is going to be more restrictive to your potential mental models than whether or not language X uses floats as its number type

elements.text: Doing most of your computing in a system that still uses a teletype metaphor at its core is going to be more restrictive to your potential mental models than whether or not language X uses floats as its number type

text:
Hello All,

I am here to make a (re)introduction. And the reason is that I am looking for a new adventure. For the last one year I have been working in Robocorp as a Senior Software Engineer. I was leading the AI / ML part. I have built the entire training, ops and related parts their. Here are two interesting projects I had worked on there

1.> Identifying and classifying UI elements from a screen shot- I started with a single line of brief. I created the detail problem statement, defined the methodologies, oversaw the data gathering process, trained, tested and debugged classical and deep learning based algorithms, I managed to out-perform the most advanced algorithm publicly available in this domain, and finally I integrated this in the main product as a PoC. Also, I crafted a detailed documentation of the entire process. And I did all this in less than 6 months of time.

2.> Creating a general purpose DSL to DSL translator- I have worked on this project starting from this July. I started with a minimal brief, I studied the two DSLs, formulated the problem, explored the domains by writing code, and then started to architect the system. I chose Go for the sake of speed and portability. I created the first PoC of this project in less then three months time. The demonstration to selected clients were immensely successful and it spawned a whole new team and became one of the main project in the company. Here also, I created, updated, and maintained a detailed documentation.

What can I do?

I am a Full Stack Engineer in its true sense. I can really work on the whole spectrum.

You have a ReactJS stack? I can work on that (HTML5, CSS3? yes of course if needed I can get my hands dirty and chip in)
You have a back-end in Python (Flask, FastAPI, Django...) or Go? (postgres? MySql? MongoDB? Cassandra... ?) I can write APIs, Scale it, maintain it, guide junior engineers to work... You name it.
You need to create a complex and demanding Data Engineering pipeline? Involving (but not limited to), Kafka, Beam, Spark, Redis, Snowflake, Graph Data Base...? I am the person who can do it
You need Machine Learning engineer? Formulate the problem, explore different avenues, Create PoC and test it against real world data (mainly CV and NLP), Work on the promising direction to bring it to production (Very few ML projects see the daylight because often the skills to bring them to production is lacking)? I will be your go-to person in the company for that
I am also good with Dev-Ops. I know my ways around Ansible, Docker, K8S.
I have always worked in Startups and I am no stranger to high pressure situation and the need for speed of delivery (yet keeping the quality)


So as you can see, I am truly fullstack in every sense you think about. I am a passionate engineer with a very easy going personality. I thrive with hard challenges. I am an excellent team player. I am also very familiar with Agile and Scrum based ways of working.

I am attaching my Resume with this message. I am looking forward to hearing from you and jump in an amazing adventure together.
